feedback.text: While the Proposals made within the Digital Fairness Act are certainly a step in the right direction, I feel like it misses the core of the Problem when it comes to the state of Digital Markets: The matter of ownership. While it used to be that the Media that the consumer owns is theirs to enjoy as long as they want, now it has become standard for companies to be able to revoke access to said Media at any time. The rights holders will then say that the consumer technically never bought the Product, even when it was being advertised as a purchase. This is done not because these Companies have to fear existential monetary losses, but to deprive the customer of any and all power over the Art they consume. This problem has recently gained a lot of traction as EU Citizens united behind the Stop Destroying Videogames Initiative, which managed to reach over 1.4 million signatures. I personally have countless stories of games I've made memories in that will be with me for the rest of my life. Sadly, for a lot of them, memories will be all I have left, since Gamemodes have been deactivated or whole games have been rendered unplayable, even though I bought them in retail, but had to activate them on an online platform. It is time for the EU to take a stand. No longer can a piece of Media be advertised as "being sold" when the rights holder can revoke access at any point. Media that was already sold to customers has to be made available indefinitely. Interactive Media, such as Games, need to have an "end-of-life" Plan so that if a company doesn't see a monetary incentive to keep the support of a game up, they have to at least give the consumer the necessary tools to keep the game in a functional state, with all of its features.
